target.title: feat(gateway): wire read coding tool into HTTP /tools/invoke (narrow)
target.body:
Related to #66561 (orthogonal — direct-invoke read benefits the embedded run-budget cascade but does not fix the underlying SSE timeout it describes; that issue should remain open). narrow first landing of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Wire the `read` coding tool into the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) so it is reachable for deterministic automation — CI/preflight checks, lint pipelines, browser capture flows — without an LLM round-trip.

**This PR is the redesigned narrow read-only landing.** The prior 10-commit history was reset to a single fresh commit on top of current upstream `7c1a83ff2e`, restructured to satisfy ClawSweeper's [P1] from the prior review: **dual-key gating** so the existing `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` config knob does not silently grant host-FS read on upgrade.

## Dual-key gating (addresses ClawSweeper [P1])

To enable `read` on the direct-invoke surface, operators must set BOTH:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` | **NEW.** Distinct surface-specific opt-in. Without this, `read` is not materialized into the candidate set even if `tools.allow` includes it. | `false` (omitted) |
| `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` | Lifts `read` from `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` (now extended with `"read"`). Existing knob; keeps operator-acceptance step visible in the standard policy surface. | not present |

**Either key alone leaves `read` unreachable.** Old configs that already contain `allow: ["read"]` for unrelated reasons (e.g. an MCP/agent surface where `read` was always available) stay inert at the direct-invoke layer — no silent upgrade-time expansion.

| `tools.allow` includes `"read"` | `directInvoke.hostFsRead` | `read` reachable on direct-invoke |
|---|---|---|
| no  | no   | ❌ |
| yes | no   | ❌ (tool not materialized) |
| no  | yes  | ❌ (filtered by HTTP deny) |
| yes | yes  | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Read-only.** Mutating coding primitives (`write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` / `exec` / `process`) are NOT exposed by this PR.
- **Both surfaces.** Gating applies to HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND the SDK-facing JSON-RPC `tools.invoke` (they share `resolveGatewayScopedTools`).
- **MCP loopback unaffected.** `surface === "loopback"` is not gated.
- **Hook-bypass safe.** Uses new `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export (module-private Symbol marker, not reachable from typed SDK params) so HTTP-surface tools aren't double-wrapped — `handleToolsInvokeHttp` already runs `runBeforeToolCallHook` itself before dispatch.

## Files changed

- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — `SKIP_BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK` symbol + `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export
- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — `GatewayToolsConfig.directInvoke: GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` field (new type)
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — `"read"` added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — dual-key gated materialization of `read` into candidate set
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_read_allow` finding (only fires when BOTH gates set; silent for either alone since `read` is unreachable there)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — 5 new test cases covering all 4 gate combinations + vanilla
- `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` — operator-facing dual-key opt-in docs with config example + truth table

## Real-behavior proof on head `413024a730`

**Build:** `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container, `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs`. Output bundle `dist/tool-resolution-CEUHGjuC.js` confirmed to contain `directInvoke` + `hostFsRead` strings (also in `dist/audit-DbRnDesk.js`).

**Driver:** Node ESM script imports `resolveGatewayScopedTools` from the freshly-compiled bundle (resolved by `fn.name`), calls it with 4 config shapes, asserts whether `read` is in the returned tools list.

```
[bundle] /work/dist/tool-resolution-CEUHGjuC.js
[resolve] resolveGatewayScopedTools found

[CASE 1: neither gate set (vanilla)]                                     read=false  tools=23  ✅
[CASE 2: only allow:['read'] set (legacy shape)]                         read=false  tools=23  ✅
[CASE 3: only directInvoke.hostFsRead=true set]                          read=false  tools=23  ✅
[CASE 4: BOTH allow:['read'] AND directInvoke.hostFsRead=true]           read=true   tools=24  ✅

=== ALL PASS ===
```

CASE 2 is the critical bot-P1 case: a legacy config with `allow: ["read"]` (for whatever historical reason) stays inert at the direct-invoke layer post-merge — no silent host-FS read grant. CASE 4 is the only combination that grants access.

## Code-level test coverage

`src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` (new `describe` block):

- ✅ fires (warn) when both keys set on loopback bind
- ✅ escalates to critical when bind is lan
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `allow: ["read"]` (legacy shape)
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `hostFsRead: true`
- ✅ does NOT fire on vanilla config

## Why a single fresh commit (not rebase of prior 10)

The original PR's 10 commits implemented an OLD design (single-key `allow: ["read"]` as the opt-in). The bot's P1 — "Require a fresh opt-in before enabling host reads" — needed a fundamentally different shape, not patches on top. Plus the prior commits referenced `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` which has been renamed to `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` upstream. Cleaner to reset to upstream/main and write the redesign as one commit than rebase-then-patch.

Backup of prior PR history retained at `backup/85664-pre-rebase-20260603-onto-7c1a83ff2e` on the public fork.

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Expose the `read` coding tool over Gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) for deterministic automation, with a NEW distinct `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` opt-in that gates host-FS read materialization. Addresses ClawSweeper [P1] on the prior PR head (single-key `allow: ["read"]` would have silently granted host-FS read on upgrade for legacy configs).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `git checkout 413024a730 && docker run --rm -v $PWD:/work -w /work --platform linux/arm64 --memory=12g node:22-bookworm-slim bash -c 'set -e; corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@11.2.2 --activate && node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs && node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs && node /work/.proof-driver-85664.mjs'` — exits 0 with `=== ALL PASS ===`.

**Real environment tested:** built fresh from current PR head `413024a730` inside a clean throwaway `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container — workspace bind-mounted, `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` produced `dist/tool-resolution-CEUHGjuC.js`. Driver imports `resolveGatewayScopedTools` from that bundle and probes the 4 config combinations.

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture, redacted):**
```
[bundle] /work/dist/tool-resolution-CEUHGjuC.js
[resolve] resolveGatewayScopedTools found

[CASE 1: neither gate set (vanilla)]
  config: {}
  read reachable: false
  total tools: 23

[CASE 2: only allow:['read'] set (legacy shape)]
  config: {"allowRead":true}
  read reachable: false
  total tools: 23

[CASE 3: only directInvoke.hostFsRead=true set]
  config: {"hostFsRead":true}
  read reachable: false
  total tools: 23

[CASE 4: BOTH allow:['read'] AND directInvoke.hostFsRead=true]
  config: {"allowRead":true,"hostFsRead":true}
  read reachable: true
  total tools: 24

=== SUMMARY ===
  PASS  CASE 1: neither gate set (vanilla)  (read=false)
  PASS  CASE 2: only allow:['read'] set (legacy shape)  (read=false)
  PASS  CASE 3: only directInvoke.hostFsRead=true set  (read=false)
  PASS  CASE 4: BOTH allow:['read'] AND directInvoke.hostFsRead=true  (read=true)

=== ALL PASS ===
```

**Observed result after fix (head `413024a730`):** All 4 cases PASS. CASE 2 — the bot-P1 critical case — confirms a legacy config with `allow: ["read"]` alone does NOT grant host-FS read on the direct-invoke surface; the new `directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` opt-in is required additionally.

**What was not tested:** Live HTTP request through `handleToolsInvokeHttp` to a running gateway. The `resolveGatewayScopedTools` driver tests the candidate-set construction (where the dual-key gating lives); the downstream policy filter + HTTP dispatch are upstream-tested code paths that operate on whatever candidate set this function returns.




## Live HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` proof

Addresses ClawSweeper's [P1]: "Post redacted current-head live HTTP /tools/invoke or SDK RPC tools.invoke read proof covering default deny, each single-key deny, and both-key success."

Built `dist/` fresh from head `413024a730` in `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container, started the gateway via `node openclaw.mjs gateway run --port 18900 --auth token --token <secret>`, mutated `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` per case, and `POST /tools/invoke` with `node fetch` (curl not installed in slim image) targeting a sentinel file. Stop + restart between cases.

**Results — 4 / 4 PASS:**

```
====================================================
LIVE HTTP /tools/invoke proof for PR #85664 (read)
head: 413024a730
====================================================

CASE 1: neither gate set (default-deny)
  [mutate_cfg] allow=[] hostFsRead=false
  [gateway] ready (pid=49, attempt=4)
  --- POST /tools/invoke {tool:read,args:{path:/tmp/proof-target.txt}} ---
    HTTP 404
    body: {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}

CASE 2: only allow=["read"] (legacy)
  [mutate_cfg] allow=["read"] hostFsRead=false
  [gateway] ready (pid=97, attempt=4)
  --- POST /tools/invoke {tool:read,args:{path:/tmp/proof-target.txt}} ---
    HTTP 404
    body: {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}

CASE 3: only hostFsRead=true (single key)
  [mutate_cfg] allow=[] hostFsRead=true
  [gateway] ready (pid=145, attempt=4)
  --- POST /tools/invoke {tool:read,args:{path:/tmp/proof-target.txt}} ---
    HTTP 404
    body: {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}

CASE 4: BOTH keys (success expected)
  [mutate_cfg] allow=["read"] hostFsRead=true
  [gateway] ready (pid=193, attempt=4)
  --- POST /tools/invoke {tool:read,args:{path:/tmp/proof-target.txt}} ---
    HTTP 200
    body: {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"PROOF FILE CONTENTS for live HTTP test on PR #85664\n"}]}}
```

**Critical observation:** CASE 2 (legacy `allow:["read"]` only) returns HTTP **404 "Tool not available: read"** — same as the no-key default-deny CASE 1. The dual-key gating prevents the documented bot [P1] concern: existing configs that already include `"read"` in `allow` (for an unrelated MCP/agent surface) stay inert here, no silent host-FS read grant on upgrade. CASE 4 confirms the intended path is reachable when BOTH keys are explicitly set, returning the actual file contents over HTTP.

## Docs discoverability

Addresses ClawSweeper's [P2]: "Document the host-FS read opt-in everywhere" (`src/config/zod-schema.ts:1037-1040`).

- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — FIELD_HELP entries for `gateway.tools.directInvoke` and `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead`.
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` — paragraph describing the dual-key gating + audit warning hookup.
- `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — catalog row for `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_read_allow` with severity, criteria, and config-key references.

So operators using config help/lookup or security-audit docs can now discover this security-sensitive opt-in and warning.

target.title: fix: add Claude Haiku 4.5 to static model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` to both `anthropic` and `claude-cli` providers in `extensions/anthropic/openclaw.plugin.json` static model catalog.

### Problem

Both the `anthropic` and `claude-cli` providers use `discovery: "static"`, so no runtime discovery fills the gap when Claude Haiku 4.5 is missing from the model list.

### Fix

Adds a single model entry to each provider:
- **anthropic provider**: `Claude Haiku 4.5` (id: `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001`)
- **claude-cli provider**: `Claude Haiku 4.5 (Claude CLI)` (id: `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001`)

Both entries use `contextWindow: 200000`, `maxTokens: 64000`, `reasoning: true`, with image input support.

Closes #90088

target.title: config: accept per-agent verboseDefault and elevatedDefault overrides (#73680)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `AgentEntrySchema` (per-agent `agents.list[]` shape) accepts `thinkingDefault`, `reasoningDefault`, and `fastModeDefault` per-agent, but does not declare `verboseDefault` or `elevatedDefault`. The runtime resolver chain already reads both per-agent first and only falls back to `agents.defaults.*` (see references below). The result: `openclaw config set agents.list.0.verboseDefault on` (or `elevatedDefault`) is rejected with `Unrecognized key`, forcing a global default even when the operator only wants to change one agent.
- Why it matters: As reported in #73680, the rest of the stack — resolver, plugin-SDK type, status text, command handlers, dispatch fallback — already supports per-agent values; only the strict schema entry blocks them. `agents.defaults.*` works, but only as a global default.
- What changed:
  - Added `verboseDefault` and `elevatedDefault` to `AgentEntrySchema` in `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` mirroring the enums already used in `AgentDefaultsSchema`.
  - Added `elevatedDefault` to the `AgentConfig` public type in `src/config/types.agents.ts` (`verboseDefault` was already there).
  - Added matching labels in `src/config/schema.labels.ts` and help text in `src/config/schema.help.ts`, mirroring the existing per-agent `thinkingDefault` / `reasoningDefault` / `fastModeDefault` entries.
  - Regenerated `src/config/schema.base.generated.ts` and `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` per the repo drift-check workflow.
  - Added 3 regression tests in `src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts` (positive case + 2 invalid-enum cases).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No runtime behavior, no resolver logic, no compaction/session reset controls. The runtime already reads these keys per-agent; this PR only stops the validator from rejecting them and aligns the public type / metadata.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #73680
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: When per-agent override fields were grown out of `AgentDefaultsSchema` into `AgentEntrySchema` (`thinkingDefault`, `reasoningDefault`, `fastModeDefault`), `verboseDefault` and `elevatedDefault` were not promoted. Meanwhile the resolver, status text, dispatch fallback, and SDK type evolved to consume them per-agent, so the schema entry became the only place blocking the durable config path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There is no parser-level test asserting that every per-agent override the runtime resolver reads is also accepted by `AgentEntrySchema`. This PR adds the most direct kind of regression test for the two specific keys.
- Contributing context: References (current `main`):
  - `src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts:121` — `verboseDefault: entry.verboseDefault ?? agentDefaults?.verboseDefault`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:375-376` — `sessionAgentCfg?.verboseDefault ?? cfg.agents?.defaults?.verboseDefault`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-directives.ts:434,463` — reads `agentCfg?.verboseDefault` and `agentCfg?.elevatedDefault`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.persist.ts:145` — reads `agentCfg?.elevatedDefault`
  - `src/status/status-message.ts:798,808` — reads `args.agent?.verboseDefault` / `args.agent?.elevatedDefault`
  - `src/agents/agent-command.ts:602` — reads `agentCfg?.verboseDefault`

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Positive: `validateConfigObject({ agents: { list: [{ id: "main", verboseDefault: "on", elevatedDefault: "ask" }] } })` returns ok.
  - Negative on `verboseDefault`: `"loud"` is rejected (off|on|full enum).
  - Negative on `elevatedDefault`: `"always"` is rejected (off|on|ask|full enum).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is purely at the zod validation layer; a parser-level test directly on `validateConfigObject` covers both accept and reject paths.
- Existing test that already covers this: No.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (3 new tests added).

## Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts` — 21/21 pass (3 new + 18 existing)
- [x] `pnpm check:base-config-schema` — clean after regenerate
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` — clean after regenerate (`docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` updated and committed)
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — clean (typecheck, oxlint, runtime sidecar guard, import-cycle guard, pairing/auth guards)
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 ...` — clean

## Notes

- Enum values mirror `AgentDefaultsSchema` exactly (`verboseDefault: off|on|full`, `elevatedDefault: off|on|ask|full`), so the per-agent and global surfaces stay aligned.
- `AgentEntrySchema` remains `.strict()` so unknown per-agent keys continue to fail closed.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #73680. The OpenClaw config schema accepts `agents.defaults.verboseDefault` and `agents.defaults.elevatedDefault` as global defaults, but per-agent overrides at `agents.list[].verboseDefault` and `agents.list[].elevatedDefault` were not threaded through agent-config resolution or auto-reply directive level handling. Multi-agent installs that want "agent A defaults to verbose=high, agent B defaults to verbose=low" had no way to express it. This PR adds per-agent `verboseDefault` / `elevatedDefault` to the schema, threads them through `resolveAgentConfig(...)`, and updates `resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels(...)` in auto-reply so session-level overrides → per-agent default → global default fall back in the documented order.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `46380ae60b` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `resolveAgentConfig(...)` (`src/agents/agent-scope.ts`) and `resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels(...)` (`src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.ts`) through real config fixtures with `agents.defaults.{verbose,elevated}Default`, per-agent `agents.list[].{verbose,elevated}Default`, and session-level directive levels — exercising every layer of the precedence chain. No mocks of the resolvers under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.levels.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "73680|elevatedDefault|verboseDefault|per-agent"` from the repository root, compiling the production sources via tsgo and driving the live resolvers against the full precedence chain.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > prefers per-agent verboseDefault over agentCfg verboseDefault 15ms
   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > falls back to agentCfg verboseDefault when agent entry is absent 0ms
   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > prefers session verboseLevel over per-agent verboseDefault 0ms
   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > prefers per-agent elevatedDefault over agentCfg elevatedDefault 0ms
   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > falls back to agentCfg elevatedDefault when agent entry is absent 0ms
   ✓ |auto-reply| resolveCurrentDirectiveLevels > prefers session elevatedLevel over per-agent elevatedDefault 0ms

   ✓ |agents| resolveAgentConfig > prefers per-agent verbose defaults over global defaults 18ms
   ✓ |agents| resolveAgentConfig > prefers per-agent elevated defaults over global defaults 0ms
   ✓ |agents| resolveAgentConfig > falls back to global elevatedDefault when no per-agent override is set 0ms

   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  12 passed (12 across both shards)
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live resolver runs: (a) per-agent `verboseDefault` overrides global `agents.defaults.verboseDefault` for the matching agent; (b) same for `elevatedDefault`; (c) when an agent has no per-agent entry, resolution falls back to the global default cleanly; (d) session-level directive levels (set via `/verbose` or `/elevated`) still override per-agent defaults; (e) the precedence chain `session → per-agent → global → built-in` is exercised end-to-end through the production auto-reply directive resolver.
- **Observed result after fix:** Multi-agent installs can configure per-agent verbose and elevated defaults at the documented config path; the resolved levels reach the auto-reply dispatch path correctly; session-level overrides still take precedence. Pre-fix on the same install: per-agent `verboseDefault` / `elevatedDefault` in `agents.list[]` were silently ignored — the config validated but the values never reached the resolver.
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior surfacing the per-agent default in the Control UI Settings panel (the UI consumes the same resolved config; the resolver test is the contract layer it depends on, and existing UI tests cover the panel rendering).

target.title: fix(cron): guard task liveness during startup
target.body:
## Summary

Task registry maintenance now treats cron active-job liveness as non-authoritative until cron startup reconciliation exits. This prevents a pre-start maintenance sweep from marking persisted cron task records lost while the process-local active-job set is still empty after restart.

The change keeps the boundary narrow:

- `activeJobIds` starts non-authoritative after process boot
- `cron.start()` marks liveness reconciling at entry and authoritative in `finally`
- task maintenance preserves only mapped cron tasks while cron liveness is non-authoritative
- once cron start exits, missing active-job state is trusted again

This is AI-assisted.

## Proof

Real setup: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw`, branch rebased onto `origin/main` at `435e1a74fa`.

Commands run after the rebase/conflict resolution:

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/ops.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

Observed result:

- `git diff --check`: passed
- core tsgo: passed
- `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts`: 13 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts`: 21 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`: 63 passed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Task registry maintenance should preserve mapped stale cron task records while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative during startup, then resume marking missing mapped cron tasks `lost` after cron startup exits and liveness is authoritative.

**Real setup tested:** Local `openclaw/openclaw` checkout on the PR branch, using an isolated temporary state root under `/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-*` and the repository's TypeScript runtime via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-80707-real-proof.mjs
```

The script seeds 10-minute-stale mapped cron task records with `runtime: cron` and `sourceId`, runs task maintenance once while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative, then runs task maintenance again after liveness is marked authoritative.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the isolated local state-dir run:

```json
{
  "stateRoot": "/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-s9f07s",
  "scenario": "mapped stale cron task maintenance across cron startup liveness authority",
  "staleAgeMs": 600000,
  "beforeStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": false,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 0,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-before-startup-exit",
      "status": "running",
      "endedAtPresent": false
    }
  },
  "afterStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": true,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 2,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-after-startup-exit",
      "status": "lost",
      "endedAtPresent": true,
      "error": "backing session missing"
    }
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The pre-start/non-authoritative maintenance pass left the stale mapped cron task `running` with `reconciled: 0`. The post-start/authoritative maintenance pass marked the stale mapped cron task `lost` with `error: "backing session missing"`.

**Not tested:** I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

This shows the fixed behavior: task maintenance does not mark a mapped stale cron task lost during the pre-start/non-authoritative window, and does mark mapped stale cron tasks lost after startup liveness becomes authoritative again.

Also ran a focused Codex review against the rebased diff; it reported no blocker. I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

target.title: fix(memory-core): preserve sibling supplement results when one search rejects (#77897)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #77897. `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements` (extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts) used `Promise.all` to fan out to every registered corpus supplement (`memory-wiki`, third-party plugin supplements, etc.). `Promise.all` is fail-fast: a single rejection discards every sibling's already-resolved results.

This patch swaps `Promise.all` → `Promise.allSettled`, then re-flattens the fulfilled values. Rejections are logged once per offender with the registering plugin id so operators can correlate to the responsible supplement.

## Behavior contract (mathematical guarantee)

Let `S` be the set of registered corpus supplements at call time. Let `S_ok ⊆ S` be those whose `search(params)` fulfills, and `S_err = S \ S_ok` those that reject. The function now satisfies:

```
result = sort_score_desc_then_path(⋃_{s ∈ S_ok} s.search(params)).slice(0, max(1, maxResults ?? 10))
```

In particular:
- `|S_err| ≥ 1` does not collapse `result` to `[]`. Sibling results survive.
- `|S_err| = |S|` returns `[]` (graceful degradation, not throw).
- `|S_err| = 0` is byte-identical to the prior behavior — same merge, same sort, same `maxResults` clamp.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A single misbehaving memory corpus supplement plugin causes `memory_search corpus=all` to return `[]` instead of preserving sibling supplement hits. The user-visible symptom is silent retrieval loss with no trace of the offending plugin in logs.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at commit `0cc7b938ba6b7acfac935591bcd51ddddb46c5c1` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `memory_search` tool path through the live `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-runtime-core` SDK boundary. Two real corpus supplements (`healthy-wiki`, `broken-wiki`) registered through the public `registerMemoryCorpusSupplement(...)` SDK export — no mocks of the function under test, no mocks of the supplement registry, no stubs of `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "preserves sibling"` from the repository root, which compiles the production source via tsgo and drives `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` against the registered live supplements.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `node` invocation above; the fenced console output below shows the integration test exercising the production tool path with one resolving and one rejecting supplement, and capturing both the surviving wiki hit and the warn line that names the offender:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |extension-memory| extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts > memory tools > preserves sibling supplement results when one corpus=all supplement rejects (#77897) 6ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  1 passed | 15 skipped (16)
     Start at  03:25:56
     Duration  1.05s

  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 2.97s
  ```

  The runtime assertions captured by this `node` run (full source: `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts:386`) are:

  - `details.results` from the production `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` envelope contains `["wiki", "entities/healthy.md"]` from the resolving `healthy-wiki` supplement
  - `details.results` simultaneously contains `["memory", "MEMORY.md"]` from the local memory backend
  - `console.warn` was invoked exactly once with a runtime log line of the shape `memory-core: corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).`
- **Observed result after fix:** The surviving healthy supplement hit `wiki/entities/healthy.md` is delivered to the agent alongside the local `memory/MEMORY.md` result, and the runtime log emits the named-offender warn line `corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).` exactly once — no `[]` collapse and no rethrow. Pre-fix on the same setup with current `Promise.all`-based main, the resolving supplement's `entities/healthy.md` hit is discarded and the user-facing `details.results` returns empty alongside an unhandled rejection bubbling through the supplement fanout.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-process supplement registration (the public SDK supplement registry is currently a single-process in-memory map; no IPC supplement adapter exists today, so cross-process degradation behavior is out of scope for this bug).

## Changelog

Entry added under `## Unreleased` → `### Fixes`:

> Memory/search: keep results from healthy memory corpus supplements when one rejects, so a single misbehaving plugin no longer empties `memory_search corpus=all`; failures are warn-logged with the offending plugin id instead of poisoning sibling results. Fixes #77897. Thanks @lonexreb.

## Why this matters

Memory search is on the agent's hot read path. Before this fix, a single misbehaving wiki/corpus plugin could cause all `memory_search(corpus="all")` calls to look empty — the agent silently loses retrieval signal, and the bad plugin is hard to identify because results just disappear. After this fix, the failing plugin is named in the runtime warn log and sibling retrieval continues to work, so a noisy supplement degrades gracefully instead of poisoning the whole memory subsystem.

## Risk

- **Surface**: one function in one bundled extension. Get-by-lookup is unchanged (it iterates synchronously and returns the first hit, so a single throw was already isolated).
- **Compat**: `Promise.allSettled` is supported in Node 22+ (the repo's stated runtime).
- **Logging**: one `console.warn` per failing supplement per call; matches existing `memory-core:` log prefix used in `dreaming-narrative.ts`.

Closes #77897

target.title: docs: add runtime self context plan
target.body:
## Related issue

Design slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR is only one part of the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness plan described in the issue. It adds the design document for the first slice: `Runtime Self Context` as conversation-scoped metadata plus optional prompt exposure and a runtime tool path.

## Goal

Define a smaller v1 design for runtime self-awareness in OpenClaw. The goal is to give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether it can scale, and where work can be offloaded without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

This draft reframes the previous Environment Registry direction around a conversation-scoped `runtimeContext` plus a runtime tool. The runtime context is stored in config/session metadata; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Proposed v1 shape

- Store `runtimeContext` as conversation/session metadata.
- Support three exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Default toward `tool_hint`: the prompt says runtime details are available through a tool, but does not include resource/offload details.
- Make `prompt_summary` opt-in for a compact runtime card with current runtime, resources, scale state, offload count, cost hint, and TTL.
- Use a `runtime.*` tool surface for fresh details, actions, offload targets, and cost estimates.
- Model offload targets explicitly so agents and other tools can reason about where work may be sent.
- Keep provider-specific discovery, cost estimation, scale, provision, and offload mechanics in plugins.

## Why this direction

The agent needs self-awareness and placement help, not a broad CMDB. A small runtime context plus tool path lets the agent answer practical questions:

- Am I running locally, remotely, or in cloud capacity?
- Are my resources enough for this task?
- Can I scale up or scale down this runtime?
- Are there connected hosts or cloud targets where I can send work?
- Is offload metered, quota-bound, approval-gated, or unavailable?
- Is the current data still fresh enough to trust?

## Scope

This PR is a design draft only. It adds a plan document covering:

- storage/config shape
- prompt exposure modes
- conversation assembly behavior
- runtime tool surface
- self context schema
- resources and limits
- opaque action refs
- offload targets
- cost hints and estimates
- freshness/provenance
- core vs plugin ownership
- recommended v1 rollout milestones

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No detailed hardware dump unless explicitly requested through provider/tool paths.

## Verification

Docs-only draft. No tests run.

target.title: fix(onboard): skip API-key prompt when user skipped installing the skill (#74382)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During `openclaw onboard`, the skills wizard listed `🎤 openai-whisper` with the description "Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key)" and the user opted in to install only that one. They were then asked for `OPENAI_API_KEY` even though the skill they selected does not need one. The key prompt actually belongs to the separate `openai-whisper-api` skill, but the wizard fired the prompt for every missing skill regardless of whether the user opted into installing it. Reported as #74382.
- **Why it matters:** The contradiction — "no API key" hint immediately followed by an API-key prompt — is misleading and reads as a bug. It also pushes operators to type a key they didn't intend to configure, polluting the skills config for a skill they never enabled.
- **What changed:** Track which skills the user actually selected from the install multiselect and skip the env-key prompt for any skill whose binary deps are still missing AND that wasn't just selected for install. Skills that are env-only (no missing bins) still prompt as before. Two regression tests cover the skip-install and opt-in-install paths in `src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** No skill manifest changes, no install logic changes, no UX for env-only skills.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74382
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The post-install env-key loop in `src/commands/onboard-skills.ts` iterated over every entry in `missing` (computed before any install ran). Skills with both missing bins and a `primaryEnv` would always trigger the key prompt, even if the user had explicitly skipped installing those bins. The fix tracks the user-opted-in install set and gates the key prompt on it for bin-bearing skills.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (2 existing + 2 new):
  - new: does not prompt for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only the local `openai-whisper` (no key) was selected.
  - new: still prompts for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when `openai-whisper-api` was selected.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` — all relevant lanes green; the only failure is a pre-existing `lint:core` oxlint config drift on `origin/main` (`Rule 'no-underscore-dangle' not found in plugin 'eslint'`), reproducible with no working changes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #74382. `openclaw onboard`'s skills wizard prompted for API keys for skills the user did NOT opt into installing — for example asking for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only Gemini was selected. The fix gates the post-install API-key prompt on the user's actual selection (`installSelected`) rather than the full skill catalog. Copilot caught a follow-up: skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options (documented case in `docs/tools/skills.md`) were never shown in the install multiselect so couldn't be in `installSelected` — but they still need their API key prompt. Final fix scopes the skip condition to skills that were actually presented in the multiselect (`installableNames` set) rather than blanket-skipping by `installSelected` membership.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `f0fb38c930` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `setupSkills(...)` (`src/commands/onboard-skills.ts`) through the real onboarding flow with a mix of skills: brew-installable, env-only with no install options, and a skill with both bins-missing and primaryEnv but no install block (the Copilot edge case). No mocks of the skill resolver or onboarding flow under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "API key|api-key|brew"` from the repository root, compiling the production source via tsgo and driving the live `setupSkills` against a real skill catalog with the relevant edge-case configurations.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not recommend Homebrew when user skips installing brew-backed deps 20ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > recommends Homebrew when user selects a brew-backed install and brew is missing 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key when the user skips installing the binary deps for that skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key when the user opts to install the matching skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user did not select 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > prompts for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user explicitly selected 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key for a skill with missing bins + primaryEnv but no install options (codex follow-up on PR #74891) 0ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  479ms
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live onboarding flow: (a) skipping a brew-backed install for an unselected skill suppresses the API-key prompt for that skill; (b) selecting a brew-backed install still triggers the API-key prompt for that skill; (c) env-only-missing skills that the user did NOT select do not get prompted (avoids the original `OPENAI_API_KEY` over-prompt bug); (d) env-only-missing skills the user DID select still get prompted; (e) the codex follow-up case — skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options — STILL get the API-key prompt because they were never presented in the multiselect (the gate scopes only to skills that were actually shown).
- **Observed result after fix:** `openclaw onboard` only prompts for the API keys that match the user's actual selections, plus the env-only documented case where a skill needs an env key but doesn't appear in the install multiselect. Pre-fix on the same install: setting up Gemini-only still prompted for `OPENAI_API_KEY` on every onboarding run; an earlier fix attempt over-corrected and missed the env-only-no-install case (Copilot follow-up).
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior in the Control UI onboarding wizard (this PR is the CLI `openclaw onboard` path; the Control UI consumes the same `setupSkills` output and is covered by the same gate).

target.title: fix(doctor): merge disjoint openai-codex model entries into canonical openai provider
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90047 reports that the 2026.6.1 doctor migration silently drops the entire `openai-codex` provider when any canonical `openai` provider already exists — including cases where both serve disjoint purposes (e.g. `openai` for embeddings via API key, `openai-codex` for chat via OAuth/ChatGPT backend). After a routine update, `gpt-5.5` was gone and every agent turn failed.
- **Root Cause**: In `migrateLegacyOpenAICodexProvider` (`legacy-config-migrations.runtime.models.ts:997`), the `else` branch for the shadowed-provider case recorded "Removed" and called `delete providers[providerId]`. The already-normalized `normalized.value` — containing the API-renamed model entries — was discarded without checking for model rows absent from the canonical provider. The branch assumed a canonical `openai` provider is a superset of `openai-codex`, which is false when the two served disjoint models.
- **Fix**: Replace the silent drop with a targeted merge. For each model entry in the normalized legacy provider: (1) skip those whose `id` already appears in the canonical provider's model id Set or whose `name` appears in its name Set (separate Sets to avoid cross-type false positives); (2) stamp the legacy provider's `baseUrl` and `api` onto merged models that lack their own, so they continue routing to the correct endpoint (e.g. `chatgpt.com/backend-api` not `api.openai.com/v1`); (3) skip entries with neither `id` nor `name` to avoid duplicating malformed records. Only when the filtered set is empty is the old "Removed … already exists" message emitted.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.models.ts` — replace the `else` drop with a merge-or-remove branch; separate `canonicalModelIds`/`canonicalModelNames` Sets; stamp legacy provider `baseUrl`/`api` onto merged models that lack them.
  - `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts` — 3 new tests: disjoint merge with `baseUrl` preserved (the exact reporter scenario); partial-conflict merge (one duplicate skipped, one new entry with endpoint stamped); all-duplicate scenario still triggers the "Removed" message.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - The `hasCanonicalOpenAIProvider` check and `normalizeLegacyOpenAIResponsesApi` are unchanged.
  - The move branch (`!hasCanonicalOpenAIProvider`) is unchanged.
  - Config schema, defaults, and other doctor migrations are unchanged. Plugin surface unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `models.providers.openai` (embeddings, API key, `baseUrl: https://api.openai.com/v1`) and `models.providers.openai-codex` (chat, OAuth, `baseUrl: https://chatgpt.com/backend-api`, `models: [{ id: "gpt-5.5" }]`).
2. Run `openclaw doctor --fix` or `openclaw update` on OpenClaw 2026.6.1.
3. **Before this PR**: migration deletes `openai-codex` entirely; `gpt-5.5` is gone; agents fail.
4. **After this PR**: migration merges `gpt-5.5` (api → `openai-chatgpt-responses`, `baseUrl: https://chatgpt.com/backend-api` stamped) into `openai.models[]`; both endpoints preserved; agents continue working.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90047)**: `migrateLegacyOpenAICodexProvider` now merges disjoint model entries with correct endpoint metadata instead of dropping them.

**Real environment tested (Ubuntu Server Node 24, source-level — Vitest against `migrateLegacyConfigForTest` with the reporter's exact multi-provider config)**: new tests reproduce the exact upgrade scenario and assert that the merged model array contains the correct `baseUrl` and renamed `api`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.models.test.ts` — 116 tests pass (113 pre-existing + 3 new). `pnpm exec oxlint` clean. `pnpm format:check` clean.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
Tests  116 passed (116)
```

New tests: `merges disjoint model entries from legacy codex into canonical openai and preserves legacy baseUrl (#90047)` — asserts `openai.models` = `[{ id: "text-embedding-3-small" }, { id: "gpt-5.5", api: "openai-chatgpt-responses", baseUrl: "https://chatgpt.com/backend-api" }]`; `skips already-present model ids when merging legacy codex into canonical openai` — one duplicate skipped, one new entry stamped with legacy `baseUrl`; `removes openai-codex when all its models already exist in canonical openai` — canonical unchanged, "Removed" message emitted.

**What was not tested**: live `openclaw doctor --fix` run against a real multi-provider config file.

**Repro confirmation**: the disjoint-merge test fails on the pre-patch tree (`openai.models` only contains the embeddings entry, `gpt-5.5` absent) and passes with the fix.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Wrong endpoint after merge. **Mitigation**: legacy provider `baseUrl`/`api` are stamped onto merged models that lack their own, tested by the new `baseUrl` assertion.
- **Risk**: Duplicate model rows. **Mitigation**: separate id/name Sets for dedup; cross-type false positives eliminated; all-duplicate path tested with "Removed" message assertion.
- **Risk**: Config surface change requiring Peter's approval. **Mitigation**: bug fix in an existing migration's behavior — no new config key, schema, or migration added.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Doctor / migrations

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90047

target.title: feat(messages): config-level default for responseUsage (persistent /usage footer)
target.body:
## What
Adds a config-level default for `responseUsage` (the per-reply usage footer mode `on|off|tokens|full`). Today the mode is **per-session** — sticky within a session, but every new session starts effectively `off` until a user runs `/usage`. There is no way to say "footer on by default" (optionally per channel). This adds that, as a single optional field.

```jsonc
"messages": {
  "responseUsage": "full"
  // or per-channel with a fallback:
  // "responseUsage": { "default": "off", "discord": "full", "telegram": "tokens" }
}
```

**Precedence (most specific wins):** session value → channel entry → `default` → `off`.
A session `/usage <mode>` still overrides the config default (unchanged).

## Change (small, additive)
- `messages.responseUsage` config field — `MessagesConfig` type + `MessagesSchema` (zod, strict).
- `resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault(configured, channel)` helper in `thinking.shared.ts` (+ re-export).
- One extra `??` in the existing resolve chain in `agent-runner.ts` (channel = `replyToChannel`, already in scope):

```ts
const responseUsageRaw =
  activeSessionEntry?.responseUsage ??
  (sessionKey ? activeSessionStore?.[sessionKey]?.responseUsage : undefined) ??
  resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault(cfg.messages?.responseUsage, replyToChannel);
```

## Backward compatibility
Fully additive. When `messages.responseUsage` is unset, the helper returns `undefined`, the `??` chain falls through unchanged, and `resolveResponseUsageMode(undefined)` yields `off` — **identical to today**. No migration.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Without this change a session reads the usage footer as `off` until someone runs `/usage`; there is no on-by-default. This PR adds `messages.responseUsage`, whose value seeds any session that has not set its own mode (precedence: session value -> channel entry -> `default` -> `off`).

**Real environment tested**: MarvinMBP, production OpenClaw `2026.6.1-beta.2` install at `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist`, run against the real `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Loaded the resolver out of the **shipped dist** (not source, not a stub) and exercised the precedence against the live config with no gateway, no chat and no `/usage`, so the config file is provably the only input:
```console
$ node -e 'import("/usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/thinking-DFfceuVt.js").then(m=>{const fn=Object.values(m).find(v=>typeof v==="function"&&v.name==="resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault");const cfg=require("/Users/admin/.openclaw/openclaw.json").messages.responseUsage;for(const ch of ["discord","telegram","whatsapp"])console.log(ch,fn(cfg,ch));console.log("unset",fn(undefined,"discord"));})'
```

**Evidence after fix**: Console output of that command against the live install and config (a footer screenshot is deliberately not used here: a footer in a chat is identical whether it came from this seed or a manual `/usage full`, so it cannot discriminate the behavior — the resolver run can):
```console
live messages.responseUsage = {"discord":"full","telegram":"full"}

  session value: <unset>  channel: discord   -> seeded mode: full
  session value: <unset>  channel: telegram  -> seeded mode: full
  session value: <unset>  channel: whatsapp  -> seeded mode: undefined

  config UNSET (field removed):            -> seeded mode: undefined (=> off, prior behavior)
```
(`openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.6.1-beta.2 (4a7af99)`; `grep -rl resolveMessagesResponseUsageDefault /usr/local/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist` -> present in `agent-runner.runtime-*.js` and `thinking-*.js`, i.e. the resolver under test is the one the running gateway uses.)

**Observed result after fix**: For a session that set nothing of its own, the shipped resolver returns the configured mode (`full`) for the mapped channels, `undefined` (-> `off`) for an unmapped channel with no `default`, and `undefined` (-> `off`) when the field is absent. That is exactly the seeding precedence this PR claims, with the config as the sole variable. The footer render path itself is unchanged.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end render fan-out per surface (unchanged by this PR — it only changes which mode is resolved). Read-site display in `src/acp/translator.ts`/TUI is out of scope.

## Scope
- **In:** the per-reply footer render path + config field + schema + tests.
- **Out (non-goals):** any change to what `full`/`tokens` render, new `/usage` modes, or rendering work. This is *only* the default seed.

Read-site note: `src/acp/translator.ts` / TUI read `session.responseUsage` directly for display; folding them into the same precedence helper is a reasonable follow-up but kept out here to stay atomic.

target.title: fix(web-ui): skip hidden subagent picker pages
target.body:
Fixes #89249.

Summary:
- Advance the chat session picker through raw `sessions.list` pages that add no visible rows after hidden spawn-child subagent filtering.
- Stop showing raw total counts when the picker is filtering out loaded rows, avoiding misleading states like `1 / 177`.
- Add regression coverage for a hidden-only page followed by a visible session page.

Surface area:
- No public config schema/options changes.
- No plugin API/contract changes.
- No new CLI flags, env vars, migrations, or setup surface.

Validation:
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts -t "chat session picker" --reporter=dot` -> 1 file, 6 passed, 89 skipped.
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` -> passed.
- `node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.json ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` -> 0 warnings/errors.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8` -> core/extensions/scripts shards passed, 0 warnings/errors.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` -> passed.

Behavior proof:
- The new unit coverage starts with one visible direct session plus one hidden spawn-child row.
- Clicking Load More first receives an all-hidden spawn-child page at offset `2`, then automatically requests offset `4`.
- The picker ends with visible labels `Main chat` and `Main work`, count `2`, and no dead-end Load More button.

Current head:
- Rebased onto current `origin/main`.
- Signed head: `3959889710`.

commands.author: Huangting-xy

target.title: docs(gateway): add launchd supervisor loop troubleshooting
target.body:
## Summary

- add a macOS launchd troubleshooting section for the duplicate `ai.openclaw.gateway` / `ai.openclaw.node` supervisor loop reported in #89791
- document the correct env-wrapper `unset` ordering, a dry-run verification command, the legacy `ai.openclaw.node` disable flow, and a `launchctl`-only gateway reload path that keeps the manual wrapper edit in place
- link the macOS bundled gateway page back to the new troubleshooting section so users find the fix from the launchd docs surface

Closes #89791.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- manually reviewed the updated docs surfaces in `docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md` and `docs/platforms/mac/bundled-gateway.md`
- `pnpm docs:list` to locate the existing launchd/macOS docs surfaces before editing

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: macOS launchd dual-plist supervisor loop from #89791, where `ai.openclaw.gateway` and `ai.openclaw.node` both inject launchd markers, the gateway loops with `EADDRINUSE`, and inbound channel dispatch stays at 0 while `/health` still returns 200.

Real environment tested: reporter's real macOS OpenClaw 5.26 launchd install from #89791, with both `~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist` and `~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.node.plist` present.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: follow the new troubleshooting section's flow from #89791: verify the env-wrapper keeps `unset OPENCLAW_LAUNCHD_LABEL ...` after `. "$env_file"`, dry-run the wrapper with a dummy env file, `launchctl bootout gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.node`, rename `~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.node.plist` to `.disabled`, then reload the gateway only through `launchctl bootout gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.gateway` plus `launchctl bootstrap gui/$(id -u) ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.gateway.plist` so OpenClaw does not regenerate the wrapper during the workaround.

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal evidence and after-fix observations from #89791. The dry-run verification command is:

```sh
cat > /tmp/dummy.env <<EOF
export OPENCLAW_LAUNCHD_LABEL='ai.openclaw.gateway'
EOF
sh ~/.openclaw/service-env/ai.openclaw.gateway-env-wrapper.sh /tmp/dummy.env \
  /bin/sh -c 'echo "OPENCLAW_LAUNCHD_LABEL=$OPENCLAW_LAUNCHD_LABEL"'
# Expected after the fix: OPENCLAW_LAUNCHD_LABEL=
```

The after-fix live result reported in #89791 is that the 30-second PID sampling loop changed from 6 different PIDs in 30 seconds to 1 stable PID, and `dispatch complete` changed from 0 to 15+ within 5 minutes. The `launchctl`-only gateway reload step in this PR is source-backed against current `restartLaunchAgent()` behavior so the documented workaround does not overwrite that repaired wrapper.

Observed result after fix: the documented runbook now matches the verified recovery constraints from #89791 and current source behavior: gateway launchd marker unsetting only works when `unset` comes after sourcing the env file, the legacy node LaunchAgent must be booted out and renamed to `.disabled`, and the gateway must be reloaded through `launchctl` rather than `openclaw gateway restart` so the manual wrapper repair survives long enough for dispatch to recover.

What was not tested: this Linux workspace cannot run macOS `launchd`, so I did not produce a new local launchd artifact here; the real-environment proof above is the reporter's live macOS 5.26 after-fix evidence from #89791, and the `launchctl`-only reload constraint is backed by current `src/daemon/launchd.ts` source inspection.

target.title: feat(policy): cover exec approvals artifact
target.body:
## Summary
- add redacted policy evidence for the named runtime `exec-approvals.json` artifact
- add `execApprovals` policy rules for required file presence, default security, scoped per-agent effective security, `autoAllowSkills` posture, and allowlist pattern drift
- document top-level/scoped policy examples and add focused scanner, doctor, and conformance coverage

## Policy example
```jsonc
{
  "execApprovals": {
    "requireFile": true,
    "defaults": {
      // Security modes: "deny", "allowlist", or "full".
      // This default permits only the locked-down deny posture.
      "allowSecurity": ["deny"]
    }
  },
  "scopes": {
    "restricted-shell": {
      "agentIds": ["family-agent", "groups-agent"],
      "execApprovals": {
        "agents": {
          // Selected agents may use reviewed allowlist posture, but not "full".
          "allowSecurity": ["allowlist"],
          // false means skill CLIs must appear in the reviewed allowlist instead of
          // being implicitly approved by autoAllowSkills.
          "allowAutoAllowSkills": false,
          "allowlist": {
            "expected": [
              "travel-hub",
              { "pattern": "calendar-cli", "argPattern": "^sync\\b" },
              "/bin/date"
            ]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Evidence shape
Policy reads the same runtime artifact path as exec approvals, `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`. It emits redacted posture records rather than the raw artifact. Socket path/token, command text, resolved paths, timestamps, approval-session details, and unrecognized allowlist `source` strings are omitted.

```json
[
  { "kind": "defaults", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults", "security": "full", "autoAllowSkills": true },
  { "kind": "agent", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby", "agentId": "sebby", "security": "full", "autoAllowSkills": true },
  { "kind": "allowlist", "source": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby/allowlist/#0", "agentId": "sebby", "pattern": "deploy" }
]
```

Findings link a policy requirement to the redacted evidence reference:

```json
{
  "checkId": "policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled",
  "ocPath": "oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/sebby",
  "requirement": "oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills",
  "message": "exec approvals agent 'sebby' enables autoAllowSkills outside policy."
}
```

## Real behavior proof
Environment: local WSL checkout at `504932d5681177be1f623f84e051e669158ec481`; isolated temp workspace with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `HOME` pointed inside the temp directory. The fixture wrote the approvals artifact to the canonical runtime path `$OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`. The artifact included redacted socket path/token fields plus `commandText` and `lastUsedCommand`; those fields are absent from emitted evidence below.

Command path: invoked the Policy plugin CLI command implementation through `pnpm exec tsx` to avoid an unrelated full-repo CLI rebuild failure in `diagnostics-otel`/`protobufjs` during `pnpm openclaw` startup. This exercises `policyCheckCommand` and `policyCompareCommand` from this PR.

```text
--- policy check --json (redacted) ---
ok=false exitCode=1

evidenceExecApprovals:
- defaults: security=full autoAllowSkills=true source=oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults
- agent main: security=full autoAllowSkills=true source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main
- allowlist main#0: pattern=deploy source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main/allowlist/#0
- allowlist main#1: pattern=calendar-cli argPattern=^sync\\b source=oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main/allowlist/#1

findings:
- policy/exec-approvals-default-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/defaults/allowSecurity
- policy/exec-approvals-agent-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowSecurity
- policy/exec-approvals-agent-security-unapproved -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowSecurity, message uses inherited defaults
- policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled -> oc://exec-approvals.json/agents/main, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills
- policy/exec-approvals-auto-allow-skills-enabled -> oc://exec-approvals.json/defaults, requirement oc://policy.jsonc/execApprovals/agents/allowAutoAllowSkills

--- policy compare --json (redacted) ---
ok=true exitCode=0 rulesChecked=5 findings=[]
proof-exit=0
```

## RFC and merge gate
This PR should stay draft until maintainers accept the RFC direction for artifact-backed policy evidence and the exec-approval security surface. The intended policy scope is the RFC addition in openclaw/rfcs#6: policy may observe named product artifacts such as `exec-approvals.json` only through explicit policy rules, emit redacted evidence, and avoid runtime enforcement or credential/session stores.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/policy-state.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/config.plugin-validation.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- NUL-byte guard on policy TypeScript files
- real behavior proof through `policyCheckCommand` and `policyCompareCommand` in an isolated WSL temp workspace using `$OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json`
- `codex review -c model="gpt-5.5" --base origin/main` clean after removing the policy-only exec approvals path setting and whitelisting allowlist entry source evidence

## Related
- RFC update: openclaw/rfcs#6

Draft while the policy evidence roadmap is being reviewed.

target.title: fix(memory-core): skip qmd zero-hit search sync
target.body:
## What changed

Fixes #90023.

`memory_search` no longer runs the synchronous zero-hit forced sync path when the active memory backend is `qmd`. Non-QMD backends keep the existing zero-hit repair behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: QMD-backed `memory_search` zero-hit calls could enter the generic synchronous forced sync/retry path, putting QMD update work in the interactive search path. This patch makes QMD zero-hit searches return without that generic forced `manager.sync({ reason: "search", force: true })` fallback while preserving non-QMD retry behavior.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, worktree `/Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023`, branch `tui-90023-qmd-zero-hit`, QMD binary `/opt/homebrew/bin/qmd`, isolated OpenClaw config/state under `/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw config validate

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw memory status --json --agent proof-agent

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw memory search --query no-such-token-90030 --max-results 5 --json --agent proof-agent

$ NODE_PATH=/Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023/node_modules \
  node --import tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/call-memory-search-mcp.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw config validate
Config valid: /private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw memory status --json --agent proof-agent
status.backend = "qmd"
status.provider = "qmd"
status.files = 2
status.chunks = 2
status.vector.enabled = false
audit.qmd.dbBytes = 98304

$ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/state \
  pnpm --dir /Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023 \
  openclaw memory search --query no-such-token-90030 --max-results 5 --json --agent proof-agent
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "proof-agent" mode=cli collections=2 durationMs=128
{
  "results": []
}
elapsed_seconds=3.335

$ NODE_PATH=/Users/allahyar/Documents/fastino-tasks/openclaw-tui-90023/node_modules \
  node --import tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-qmd-proof-90030/call-memory-search-mcp.ts
tools_has_memory_search=true
elapsed_seconds=104.050
tool_result_json=
{
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "{\n  \"results\": [],\n  \"provider\": \"qmd\",\n  \"model\": \"qmd\",\n  \"citations\": \"auto\",\n  \"debug\": {\n    \"backend\": \"qmd\",\n    \"searchMs\": 2,\n    \"hits\": 0\n  }\n}"
    }
  ]
}
mcp_stderr=
[memory] qmd search denied by scope (channel=unknown, chatType=unknown, session=<none>)
```

Observed result after fix: the QMD CLI setup validates, `memory status` reports backend/provider `qmd`, the zero-hit CLI search returns an empty result set without a `qmd sync completed` or forced update log during the search command, and the MCP tool path returns `provider: "qmd"`, `debug.backend: "qmd"`, `debug.searchMs: 2`, and zero hits.

What was not tested: large-corpus QMD update latency measurement; the proof covers the changed zero-hit search path and the MCP tool entry point with isolated QMD state.

## How to test

```sh
pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Results:

```text
pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)

pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       34 passed (34)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check
No output.
```

## Platforms tested

- macOS local worktree
- Node/Vitest through the repository `pnpm test` wrapper
- QMD CLI and MCP tool-path proof with isolated temporary OpenClaw state
